summary: Trendelenburg sign is positive when the pelvis drops on the opposite (healthy) side due to weakness of gluteus medius and minimus on the standing (affected) side.
Trendelenburg sign is positive when the pelvis drops on the same side as the affected hip.
Trendelenburg sign tests the strength of gluteus maximus.
The safe zone for intramuscular gluteal injection is the upper outer quadrant to avoid the sciatic nerve.
Intramuscular injections can be given safely anywhere in the gluteal region.
The sciatic nerve runs in the upper outer quadrant of the gluteal region.

The hamstrings (biceps femoris, semitendinosus, semimembranosus) originate from the ischial tuberosity and are supplied by the tibial division of the sciatic nerve, except the short head of biceps (common peroneal).
All three hamstring muscles originate from the iliac crest.
The hamstrings are supplied entirely by the common peroneal nerve.
The popliteal fossa is bounded by biceps femoris (superolaterally), semitendinosus and semimembranosus (superomedially), and the two heads of gastrocnemius (inferiorly).
The popliteal fossa is bounded by quadriceps superiorly and soleus inferiorly.
The popliteal fossa does not contain any major neurovascular structures.
